coredata.dc:title: Magnetic carbon dots based molecularly imprinted polymers for fluorescent detection of bovine hemoglobin
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Piaopiao
Xie, Dandan
Zhang, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: A simple and effective method was proposed to prepare an imprinted polymer layer at the surface of magnetic carbon dots with dopamine as the functional monomer for selectively and sensitively fluorescent recognizing bovine hemoglobin. The magnetic fluorescence imprinted polymers were investigated by Fourier-transform infrared spectra, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, and vibrating sample magnetometer. Under optimum conditions, the fluorescent intensity decreased linearly coincided with the concentration of bovine hemoglobin in the range of 0.05–16.0 μM with a detection limit of 17.3 nM. The magnetic fluorescence imprinted polymers were used for detecting bovine hemoglobin in real samples with the recoveries of 99.0–104.0%. These results indicated that a convenient method was proposed to develop fluorescent probes for rapid recognition and sensitive detection of trace proteins in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymers
Magnetic carbon dots
Fluorescent detection
Bovine hemoglobin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of the M-CDs and M-CDs@MIPs.
SEM images of the M-CDs@MIPs (a) and M-CDs@NIPs (b).
TEM images of the M-CDs (a) and M-CDs@MIPs (b).
EDX spectra of the M-CDs (a) and M-CDs@MIPs (b).
VSM magnetization curves of the M-CDs and M-CDs@MIPs.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the M-CDs@MIPs (A) and M-CDs@NIPs (B) with addition of protein BHb in PBS (pH 7.0). Insets were the Stern-Volmer plots from M-CDs@MIPs (a) and M-CDs@NIPs (b) with BHb.
Selective adsorption of the M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs toward OVA, BSA, HSA and BHb.
Analytical results of BHb in the100-fold diluted bovine urine and 1000-fold diluted bovine blood (N = 5).
Performance comparison of different methods for BHb detection.

head.ce:title: Magnetic carbon dots based molecularly imprinted polymers for fluorescent detection of bovine hemoglobin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University, Changsha 410082, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing and Chemometrics, Hunan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Mineral Cleaner Production and Exploit of Green Functional Materials in Hunan Province, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Mineral Cleaner Production and Exploit of Green Functional Materials in Hunan Province, Jishou University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University, Jishou 416000, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Chemistry Education, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jishou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and effective method was proposed to prepare an imprinted polymer layer at the surface of magnetic carbon dots with dopamine as the functional monomer for selectively and sensitively fluorescent recognizing bovine hemoglobin. The magnetic fluorescence imprinted polymers were investigated by Fourier-transform infrared spectra, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, and vibrating sample magnetometer. Under optimum conditions, the fluorescent intensity decreased linearly coincided with the concentration of bovine hemoglobin in the range of 0.05–16.0 μM with a detection limit of 17.3 nM. The magnetic fluorescence imprinted polymers were used for detecting bovine hemoglobin in real samples with the recoveries of 99.0–104.0%. These results indicated that a convenient method was proposed to develop fluorescent probes for rapid recognition and sensitive detection of trace proteins in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Molecular imprinting technology which is an effective way to form recognition sites with a predetermined selectivity for template molecules has attracted widespread attention [1]. The molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) as the adsorbent material have many advantages involving ease of synthesis, good chemical/mechanical stability in environments and low preparation cost. The conventional MIPs are usually prepared by precipitation polymerization and bulk polymers [2], in which it is difficult to extract the templates from the MIPs because the recognition sites are embedded deeply in the MIPs. The surface imprinting technique provides an alternative way to overcome the limitation by localizing the cavities at the surface or in the proximity of the Supporting materials involving polystyrene nanoparticles [3], silica particles [4], magnetic nanoparticles [5], and quantum dots (QDs) [6].

The fluorescent QDs combined with the surface imprinting technique have been widely applied in protein detection because of the high sensitivity of QDs and high selectivity of MIPs. Ding et al. [7] proposed a MIPs optosensing material based on multi-walled carbon nanotube-QDs to detect bovine serum albumin. Antonio et al. [8] synthesized a MIPs-coated CdTe QDs nanocomposite for tryptophan recognition. Zhang et al. [9] developed fluorescent MIPs to recognize the target protein by coating MIPs layer on the surface of L-cysteine modified Mn2+ doped ZnS QDs. However, the inherent defects of those heavy metal based QDs involving potential toxicity and environmental hazards are hindering their applications. Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs) as recently emerging carbon materials have gained considerable attention due to their advantages over conventional semiconductor quantum dots involving biocompatibility, simple synthetic routes, bright fluorescence, excellent photostability, and convenient surface functionalization [10]. During the past few years, much progress has been achieved in the synthesis and application of CDs [11].

Additionally, magnetic MIPs which allow for rapid separation with the help of an external magnetic field have also attracted increasing interest in the biological and environmental science fields [12–14]. The magnetic fluorescent MIPs not only have excellent magnetic response property and fluorescence characteristics, but also exhibit high specific identification ability. Xu et al. [15] reported a MIPs based fluorescence sensor by grafting MIPs on the surface of ZnO nanorods embedded γ-Fe2O3 nanoparticles. Ming et al. [16] developed a core-shell structured composite of Fe3O4 nanoparticles and MIPs for the selective and sensitive fluorescent detection of 17β-estradiol. Li et al. [17] reported an selective surface-imprinted magnetic fluorescence bead for the quantitative determination of 17β-estradiol. Though these magnetic fluorescent MIPs possessed magnetic response and fluorescent response, they often involved with complex synthetic procedure.

In this work, we proposed a facile method to prepare magnetic fluorescent MIPs using magnetic carbon dots (M-CDs) which were synthesized by one-pot method as fluorescent source and supporting matrix, dopamine (DA) which can self-polymerize in alkaline condition to form thin polydopamine film on different kinds of materials as the functional monomer [18] and bovine hemoglobin (BHb) as the template molecule. The MIPs coated on the surface of the magnetic carbon dots provide specific binding sites for BHb, and the fluorescent intensity of MIPs is quenched when BHb is presented. The as-prepared magnetic fluorescent MIPs (M-CDs@MIPs) not only showed high sensitivity and selectivity toward BHb, but also exhibited good fluorescence properties and magnetism，which indicated its great application potential for specific recognition and accurate quantification of protein in complex matrices.

Bovine hemoglobin (BHb, molecular weight (MW) 66 kDa, isoelectric point (pI) 6.7), bovine serum albumin (BSA, MW 67 kDa, pI 4.9), ovalbumin (OVA, MW 45 kDa, pI 4.7), human serum albumin (HSA, MW 69 kDa, pI 4.8), and dopamine (DA) were purchased from Sigma Company. Triethylenetetramine (TETA, 70%) and ferric ammonium citrate (FAC) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). Acetone was used as received without further purification. All reagents were analytical reagent grade.

The morphology was assessed with scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-6700F, Japan) with the energy dispersive X-ray detector (EDX) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM1010, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using the Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermoscientific, USA). The magnetic property of the M-CDs@MIPs was characterized by a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, M27407, Lake Shore Ltd.). UV–vis spectra were performed on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, Japan). Fluorescence measurement was recorded on an F-7000 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). All the fluorescence detections were under the same conditions. The slit widths of the excitation and emission were both 10 nm, and the excitation wavelength was set at 360 nm. The emission range was set at 400–650 nm and the photo multiplier tube voltage was set at 650 V. Chromatography experiment were performed on the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with a LC2010AHT solution system (SHIMADZU, Japan). Chromatography analysis was achieved on a Spherigel C18 column (5 µm, 250 mm × 4.6 mm). The mobile phase is a 50 mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.0) with a flow rate of 0.5 ml/min. The injection sample volume is 10.0 μL and the wavelength for UV detector is set at 406 nm. All detected solutions were filtered through a 0.45 µm polytetrafluorethylene membrane before use.

The M-CDs nanoparticles were synthesized according to the previous report with some modification [19]. Firstly, 2.5 ml of TETA was mixed with 27.5 ml of water. Then 0.33 g of FAC was dissolved in the mixture. The precursor solution was sealed in a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and the autoclave was heated to 200 °C for 6 h. After that, the autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature. The obtained mixture was separated by centrifugation at the rate of 6500 r/min for 2 min to discard liquid phase. The solid phase was dispersed in 20.0 ml of acetone by ultrasonic dispersion for 3 min and then subjected to magnetic separation. This washing process was repeated for three times to remove dissociative CDs and TETA. Finally, the black products were dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 4 h.

In the experiments, 50.0 mg of M-CDs was added into 50.0 ml of PBS (pH=8.0) by ultrasonic for 3.0 min. Next, 40.0 mg of BHb was added and the mixture was stirred for 30 min. Then, 50.0 mg of DA was added. The mixture was stirred for 6 h at 25 °C. After that, the resultant M-CDs@MIPs were washed with 0.5% triton x-100, which was repeated several times until no template was detected by UV–vis spectrophotometer at 406 nm. Finally, the obtained polymer was dried under vacuum at 40 °C overnight.
The M-CDs coated with non-imprinted polymers (M-CDs@NIPs) were prepared using the same procedure but without addition of the template molecule.

The adsorption experiment was carried out to evaluate the binding capacity of M-CDs@MIPs while using M-CDs@NIPs as the control. Briefly, 2.0 mg of the M-CDs@MIPs or the M-CDs@NIPs was added into 4.0 ml of PBS (0.2 M, pH 7.0) under ultrasound for 10 min, Then BHb was added to the M-CDs@MIPs or the M-CDs@NIPs solution under oscillation for 12 min at 25 °C. The supernatant was separated by an external magnetic field and detected by UV−vis spectrophotometer at 406 nm. The adsorption capacity (Q, μM/g) was calculated by the below equation [20]:
Q =
(
C
0
− C ) V
m

In the equation, C0 and C (μM) are the initial and equilibrium concentrations of the template protein BHb, respectively. V (L) is the volume of the initial solution and m (g) is the weight of the M-CDs@MIPs or the M-CDs@NIPs.

100-fold dilution of bovine urine and 1000-fold dilution of bovine blood were used to demonstrate the applicability of the M-CDs@MIPs for specific recognition and quantitative fluorescence detection of BHb as previously reported papers [21–23]. As no BHb was detectable in the collected urine by the method, the recovery study was implemented toward the samples spiked with different BHb concentrations ranged from 0.5 μM to 1.5 μM. No further pretreatment procedures were employed in sample preparation.

The procedure for the preparation of M-CDs@MIPs was displayed in Fig. 1. Firstly, the magnetic–fluorescent hybrid M-CDs was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. Then, BHb was immobilized on the surface of M-CDs through multi-hydrogen interaction between the amino group of M-CDs and the amino acid on BHb to form M-CDs-template complex. Subsequently, a thin adherent polydopamine film was coated on the surface of M-CDs to immobilize the template protein by using DA as the monomer and cross-linker. Notably, polydopamine film possessed hydroxyl, amino and phenyl groups which can interact with BHb through hydrophobic effects and multi hydrogen bonds. Finally, the resultant composite was washed with 0.5% triton x-100 to remove the template BHb.

To optimize the M-CDs@MIPs preparation condition, 50.0 mg M-CDs, 50.0 mg of BHb, and varying DA amounts were mixed to study the influence of DA amount on the effective fluorescence of the prepared M-CDs@MIPs. As shown in Table S1, the fluorescence intensity quenching amount of the M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs toward BHb increased initially with the amount of DA increasing, and then decreased. The results showed the M-CDs@MIPs with the best imprinting effect was prepared by using 50.0 mg of DA.

FT-IR spectra of M-CDs and M-CDs@MIPs were shown in Fig. 2. The broad bands at 3393 cm−1 were attributed to the O-H and N-H. The band at 1637 cm−1 indicated by the C˭O vibrations. The band at 1400 cm−1 referred to the C-N bond. The bands of 581 cm−1 were ascribed to Fe-O bond. These bands indicated the occurrence of M-CDs in the both materials [19]. Additionally, some new bands were observed for the M-CDs@MIPs. The new band at 2890 cm−1 was attributed to the phenolic hydroxyl. The band at 1571 cm−1 was ascribed to the phenyl group on polydopamine chain. These new bands indicated that the M-CDs were successfully coated with polydopamine layer [18].

The morphologies of M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs were investigated by SEM. As displayed in Fig. 3, although both of them have rough surfaces, the M-CDs@NIPs seemed more dense and compact (Fig. 3b), and the larger-dimension pores only showed in the M-CDs@MIPs (Fig. 3a), which was attributed to the polydopamine layer provided the binding sites including the background sites and template sites in the polymerization process. After elution of the template BHb, the larger-dimension pores were leaved in the M-CDs@MIPs. The absence of the template during M-CDs@NIPs polymerization process resulted in formed background sites rather than the template imprinted sites. These results demonstrated the specific imprinted cavities were successfully formed in the M-CDs@MIPs, which is favorable for selective adsorption of the template BHb.

In order to study the size of the as-prepared particles, the M-CDs and the M-CDs@MIP were investigated with TEM in detail. TEM images of M-CDs and the M-CDs@MIP were shown in Fig. 4a and b, respectively. The TEM images showed that the M-CDs have an average dimension of approximately 12 nm, and the average size of the M-CDs@MIPs is approximately 62 nm.

Fig. 5 shows the patterns of EDX analysis of M-CDs (a) and M-CDs@MIPs (b). Data analysis confirms both of M-CDs (a) and M-CDs@MIPs are composed of C, O and Fe. But no distinct peak for N was observed. This may result from the relatively low contents and closeness to the strong C peak. And for M-CDs, the atomic percent of C, O, and Fe is 30.79%, 43.63%, and 24.75%, respectively. For the M-CDs@MIPs, the atomic percent of C, O, and Fe is 45.80%, 34.87%, and 18.43%, respectively. The difference in atomic percent can be caused by the polydopamine layer synthesized in the M-CDs@MIPs.

The magnetic properties of the M-CDs and M-CDs@MIPs were characterized by VSM. The magnetization curves from the VSM analysis were shown in Fig. 6. The saturation magnetization value of the M-CDs nanoparticles and the M-CDs@MIPs were 56.4 emu/g and 44.1 emu/g, respectively. The decrease of the magnetization value for the M-CDs@MIPs was resulted from the formation of the MIPs layer on the surface of M-CDs. The inserted picture is the dispersed solution of M-CDs@MIPs. Under the help of an external magnet, the M-CDs@MIPs were absorbed to one side within 2 min, which indicated the M-CDs@MIPs maintained sufficiently magnetic property to rapidly respond to an external magnetic field. These results indicated the M-CDs@MIPs can be effectively separated by magnetic field, which can improve the separation efficiency and simplify the experimental process.

Response time of the M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs for fluorescence detection of BHb was shown in Fig. S1. On addition of BHb in the M-CDs@MIPs solution, a decrease in fluorescence intensity occurred, and reached the constant value within 12.0 min. By contrast, the fluorescence intensity for the M-CDs@NIPs decreased at a faster rate, reaching a constant value after the incubation for 10.0 min. It was attributed to only nonspecific binding with BHb on the surface of M-CDs@NIPs. Thus 12.0 min was chosen as the optimum response time for the following experiments.

As the pH value affected the charge of the protein and the surface environment of the composite, the rebinding experiments for the M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs toward the protein at different pH values were carried out. Fig. S2A shows the effects of pH on fluorescence intensity change of the M-CDs, M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs. The fluorescence intensity increased along with an increase in pH ranged from 5.0 to 6.0. Then the fluorescence intensity showed stable in the pH value of 6.0–7.0 and decreased in pH ranged from 7.0 to 9.0. As shown in Fig. S2B, The fluorescence quenching effect for both M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs shared a similar trend to the fluorescence intensity changes. Considering BHb may be slightly denatured at higher or lower pH values, a PBS (0.2 M, pH 7.0) was used for further experiments to obtain high sensitivity.

The binding ability of M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs were investigated by the adsorption capacity analysis. When the initial concentration of BHb was 15.0 μM，the adsorption capacity of the M-CDs@MIPs toward BHb was 2.73 μmol/g. However, the adsorption capacity of the M-CDs@NIPs toward BHb was 0.68 μmol/g, which was lower than that of M-CDs@MIPs toward BHb. When the initial concentration of BHb increased to 20.0 μM, the adsorption capacity of the M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs was 2.75 μmol/g and 0.69 μmol/g, respectively. The larger adsorption capacity of M-CDs@MIPs was attributed to that the imprinted polymer shell contains the complementary imprinted cavities toward the template BHb.

To study the recognition ability of the M-CDs@MIPs, the recognition of BHb was performed at different concentrations ranged from 0.05 μM to 16.0 μM. As shown in Fig. 7, the fluorescence intensity of the M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs reduced gradually with the increasing concentration of BHb. The quench mechanism is based on the photo-induced electron transfer process [24]. Under photo-irradiation, hemeproteins (III) can directly intercept one of the charge carriers and be reduced to hemeproteins (II), which disrupts the excited electrons, causing the fluorescence quenching of the CDs. Inset a and b in Fig. 7 were plotted by the Stern–Volmer Equation [25] for the M-CDs@MIPs and M-CDs@NIPs with BHb.
F
o
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
⋅ [ Q ]
where F
0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of the polymer in the absence of BHb, F is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of BHb, K
SV is the quenching constant of Stern–Volmer and Q is the concentration of BHb. The imprinted factor (IF), which is the ratio of the Ksv,MIP and Ksv,NIP (Ksv,MIP and Ksv,NIP are the linear slopes of inset a and b, respectively), was used to evaluate the selectivity of the materials. As shown in Fig. 7 inset a, the Ksv,MIP was found to be 0.45 μM−1 and the linear range of the calibration curve was 0.05–16.0 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.9998. As a control experiment, the fluorescent response of M-CDs@NIPs toward BHb was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 inset b, the Ksv,NIP was found to 0.11 μM−1 and the linear range of BHb was 0.05–16.0 μM. Thus, the imprinting factor was calculated as 4.1, indicating that the M-CDs@MIPs had a better selectivity. The detection limit of the M-CDs@MIPs which is calculated following the 3σ IUPAC criteria (3σ/S) for BHb was calculated as 17.3 nM.

To evaluate the selectivity of the M-CDs@MIPs, The selectivity experiment was performed for BHb, BSA, HSA, and OVA with a same concentration of 2.0 μM. As show in Fig. 8A, the change in fluorescence intensity of the M-CDs@MIPs toward BHb was much larger than that toward BSA, HSA and OVA. However, the changes in fluorescence intensity of M-CDs@NIPs were not significant for BHb, BSA, HSA and OVA. This phenomenon can be explained from the following two aspects. Firstly, the imprinted sites complementary with the template BHb in shape, size and functionality were formed in the process of imprinting. Hence the template BHb could be strongly rebound by the M-CDs@MIPs and then cause the significant fluorescence quenching. However, BSA, HSA and OVA with different shapes or molecular weights, functional groups from BHb were not complementary to match with the imprinted sites, and thereby had less chance to quench the M-CDs@MIPs fluorescence. Secondly, BSA, HSA and OVA possessed with fewer carboxyl groups than that of BHb [26,27], so the electron-donating ability of these molecules were weaker, and then the electron-transfer interaction between them with CDs was much weaker.

To illustrate the utility of the strategy to sensitively detect the target protein from real samples, 100-fold dilution of bovine urine and 1000-fold dilution of bovine blood was prepared and detected by HPLC and M-CDs@MIPs. The results showed that BHb was not detected in 100-fold of bovine urine by HPLC and M-CDs@MIPs. The concentrations of BHb in the real samples detected by M-CDs@MIPs were in accord with that by HPLC, which indicated that the imprinted fluorescent determination method is reliable. To further verify the practicality of M-CDs@MIPs, the standard recovery experiment was carried out. As shown in Table 1, the recoveries for urine samples were 102.0–102.7%. And then, the bovine blood also spiked with different concentrations of BHb, attaining the recovers of 99.0–104.0%. These results suggested that the M-CDs@MIPs were successfully applied for the detection of BHb in biological samples.

Table 2 is the performance comparison of different fluorescence methods for BHb detection. MIP-QDs [24] possess the ability of specific recognition for the template protein, but it involved with complicate synthetic process. MIP-QDs [21] showed high sensitivity and selectivity, but the adsorption time was longer. Si-NP/CdTe/MIP [22] possessed the lower detection limit, higher selectivity, well-defined morphology, but the linear detection range was narrow. UCNPs/MOFs/MIP [28] showed the higher adsorption capacity, higher selectivity, and thermo-sensitivity, but it associated with high cost and complicate synthetic process, and high detection limit. The CdTe/M-R-MIPs [23] exhibited lower detection limit, but the linear detection concentration was narrow. In this work, the M-CDs@MIPs possessed high selectivity, wide detection range, simple synthetic process and separation procedure. The results revealed this method offers a simple and effective way for the detection of BHb in bovine solution.

Reusability is an important characteristic in the application of the M-CDs@MIPs. The adsorption-desorption cycles were repeated five times using the same batch of M-CDs@MIPs to study their reversible binding and release behavior in BHb solution (16.0 μM) and 1000-fold diluted bovine blood. As shown in Fig. S3, the M-CDs@MIP retained its fluorescence intensity and detection sensitivity over five cycles in both samples, which indicated that the M-CDs@MIP retained good recovery efficiency.

Reproducibility is also important for the application of M-CDs@MIPs. The reproducibility experiment was carried out by using five different batches of M-CDs@MIPs prepared with the same procedure at different times. Through three parallel determinations to every batch, the results showed the satisfactory reproducibility of M-CDs@MIPs with RSD less than 1.4%.

In summary, a novel magnetic fluorescent MIPs was synthesized successfully by using M-CDs as the fluorescent material and support material, and dopamine as the functional monomer in this paper. This novel magnetic imprinted fluorescent method offered a wide linear BHb detection range of 0.05–16.0 μM with a low detection limit of 17.3 nM. The M-CDs@MIPs showed a good recovery of BHb from 100-diluted bovine urine (102.0–102.7%) and 1000-diluted bovine blood (99.0–104.0%). With the advantage of the convenient synthesis and easy separation procedure, the M-CDs@MIPs showed the high selectivity and sensitivity, which indicated the M-CDs@MIPs are expected as a promising method for specific recognition and determination of proteins in biological fluids.

This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21767011 and 21565014), the Innovation Fund Designated for Graduate Students of Jishou University (JDY16016) and JSU Mn Zn V Collaborative Innovation Center Graduate Research Innovation (2017mzvg001).
There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.05.068.
